title: Learning doesn't have to look like school
paragraphs: Ten years ago, I was home educating both my children. It was an intense time, full of mess, laughter and tears. Life proceeded at a pace and there was rarely time to pause for thought.
When I did get a moment, however, I thought about what I was seeing unfold in front of me.

For home education turned out very differently to what I had imagined. I had thought that we would more or less do school-like things, but in a fun, child-led way. I’d thought that we would perhaps follow reading schemes and complete maths worksheets. We’d find sports teams to join, and have Spanish lessons together. We’d go along to social groups to play. I’d thought our life would be structured and I’d be able to see their progress. I thought that they’d learn similar things to their schooled peers.

It didn’t turn out like that. Partly that was because my children were firm. From early on they let me know that sitting at the kitchen table to do reading or maths was not going to be part of the plan. They showed me that there would be no ‘school hours’ with play kept for later. They showed me that their agenda was quite different to mine, and mostly their agenda involved play.

Play that took many forms over the years. There were the months when we played ‘Killing Enemies’ over and over again. There were the years of baking cakes without recipes, some of which were almost edible. There were the Duplo masterpieces that had to be kept intact for years afterwards. There were hours of playing Minecraft together. There was bouncing and swinging and climbing.

As I watched my children learning, it challenged everything that I had thought about education. For their learning appeared chaotic and non-linear. They would make sudden leaps and bounds and then seem to plateau for months. They had no concept of ‘starting with the basics’ and so they started with complexity instead. They would be fascinated by something and then completely lose interest, usually just as I had ordered extra resources to turn it into a ‘project’.

I’m trained as a developmental psychologist, and so I couldn’t help but think about all that I had learnt about child development, and the research I had done, trundling around schools in Greater London. For it occurred to me that everything I had ever learnt about child development had been in the context of school. School attendance was thought to be so much a natural part of childhood that it didn’t seem to have occurred to researchers to wonder about what effect school has on how children learn. The only research that looked at development without school was cross-cultural; looking at Guatemalan children and comparing them to North American children. That research is fascinating – but culture is important and cultural difference makes it hard to really isolate the effects of school.

The main thing that I saw that was different about my, unschooled, children, was how they saw themselves as the people in charge of their own learning. They were bursting with agency. They had never been told that play should be kept for playtime, and that real learning takes place when you do what you’re told. They did not assume that adults knew better than them, and in fact a common refrain in our household when I made a suggestion was ‘But I have a better idea…’

They are teenagers now. The mess is different but some things are the same. They still seem themselves as people who are in charge of their own learning. They are active participants in their own lives.

And that is what I think is the important difference between self-directed education and schooling. It’s not adults sitting back, or leaving them to get on with things. It’s not leaving things ‘up to chance’. It’s acknowledging that humans need to feel in control of what they are doing, and that one of the most important things that children can learn is to make choices about their own lives.

That was the starting point for my first book, and it is 99p on UK Kindle for a limited time only. Please share if you know parents who might be looking for an alternative way for their children to learn.
